title: Initial Fertility Testing
description: Comprehensive testing to understand your fertility baseline, including AMH levels, antral follicle count (AFC), hormone panels, and partner's semen analysis. This gives you and your doctor the full picture of what you're working with.

options.label: Complete comprehensive fertility workup
options.description: Full panel including AMH, AFC, HSG, blood work, and partner testing.

options.label: Basic testing first
options.description: Starting with AMH and semen analysis before expanding to other tests.

community_note: Getting comprehensive testing upfront saves time and helps your RE create the best treatment plan from the start.

title: Reproductive Endocrinologist Consultation
description: Meeting with your fertility specialist to review test results, discuss your specific situation, and explore treatment options. This is where you'll learn about different paths forward and what makes sense for your case.

options.label: Single clinic consultation
options.description: Meeting with one RE to get treatment recommendations.

options.label: Multiple clinic consultations
options.description: Getting second opinions from different REs before choosing treatment.

community_note: Don't hesitate to ask lots of questions and request clarification on anything you don't understand - this is your journey.

title: Treatment Method Decision
description: Choosing between IUI and IVF, or deciding to skip less invasive treatments and go straight to IVF. Your age, diagnosis, and personal preferences all play into this important decision.
choice_question: Should I try IUI first or proceed directly to IVF?

options.label: Try IUI first
options.description: Starting with less invasive intrauterine insemination before moving to IVF.

options.label: Skip IUI and go directly to IVF
options.description: Moving straight to IVF based on diagnosis, age, or personal preference.

community_note: Many women with low AMH, advanced age, or male factor infertility find going directly to IVF saves time and emotional energy.

title: Stimulation Protocol Start
description: Beginning your medication protocol to stimulate egg production. This typically involves daily injections and marks the official start of your IVF cycle. Your protocol is customized based on your response profile.

options.label: Antagonist protocol
options.description: Starting with stimulation meds and adding antagonist to prevent early ovulation.

options.label: Microdose lupron protocol
options.description: Using low-dose lupron with stimulation medications, often for poor responders.

options.label: Long lupron protocol
options.description: Extended lupron suppression followed by stimulation medications.

community_note: Trust your RE's protocol choice - they're selecting based on your specific response pattern and medical history.

title: Monitoring and Adjustments
description: Regular blood work and ultrasounds to track your response to medications. Your doctor will adjust dosages and timing based on how your follicles are developing and your hormone levels.

options.label: Standard monitoring schedule
options.description: Regular appointments every 2-3 days for blood work and ultrasounds.

options.label: Intensive monitoring
options.description: More frequent monitoring for complex cases or poor responders.

community_note: Don't be discouraged by slow starts - follicle growth often accelerates in the final days before retrieval.

title: Egg Retrieval
description: The procedure to collect your eggs after final trigger shot. You'll be under light sedation while your doctor uses ultrasound guidance to retrieve eggs from your follicles. The number retrieved often differs from what scans predicted.

options.label: Standard retrieval procedure
options.description: Ultrasound-guided egg collection under conscious sedation.

community_note: Empty follicles and lower-than-expected egg counts happen more often than you'd think - it's not a reflection of anything you did wrong.

title: Fertilization Method
description: Deciding how to fertilize your eggs - conventional IVF or ICSI (injecting sperm directly into eggs). This choice often depends on male factor issues, previous fertilization failures, or your doctor's recommendations.
choice_question: Should we use conventional IVF or ICSI for fertilization?

options.label: ICSI (sperm injection)
options.description: Injecting individual sperm directly into each mature egg.

options.label: Conventional IVF
options.description: Mixing sperm and eggs together for natural fertilization.

options.label: Split fertilization
options.description: Using both ICSI and conventional IVF on different portions of eggs.

community_note: ICSI is often recommended for male factor issues, but some couples choose it even with normal sperm to maximize fertilization rates.

title: Embryo Development
description: Waiting for your fertilized eggs to develop into blastocysts over 5-6 days. This is an anxious time as you receive daily updates on how many embryos are progressing. Not all fertilized eggs make it to blastocyst stage.

options.label: Standard culture to day 5
options.description: Growing embryos to blastocyst stage by day 5.

options.label: Extended culture to day 6-7
options.description: Continuing to culture slower-developing embryos an extra day or two.

community_note: The drop-off from fertilized eggs to quality blastocysts can be heartbreaking, but it's a normal part of the process that helps select the strongest embryos.

title: PGT Testing Decision
description: Choosing whether to genetically test your embryos for chromosomal abnormalities. This involves biopsying each blastocyst and can help identify the healthiest embryos, but also carries some risks and costs.
choice_question: Should I do PGT testing on my embryos?

options.label: PGT testing all embryos
options.description: Genetic testing to identify chromosomally normal embryos before transfer.

options.label: No PGT testing
options.description: Transferring embryos based on morphology grading without genetic testing.

options.label: Partial PGT testing
options.description: Testing only some embryos due to financial or other constraints.

community_note: The decision often comes down to your age, number of embryos, previous pregnancy losses, and financial considerations.

title: Transfer Preparation
description: Preparing your body for embryo transfer with medications to build your uterine lining. You'll choose between natural cycle monitoring or medicated protocols, and your lining will be carefully tracked via ultrasound.

options.label: Medicated FET protocol
options.description: Using estrogen and progesterone to artificially prepare the uterine lining.

options.label: Natural cycle FET
options.description: Using your body's natural ovulation cycle for timing the transfer.

options.label: Modified natural cycle
options.description: Natural cycle with some medication support like trigger shots.

community_note: Both medicated and natural cycles can be successful - your doctor will recommend based on your specific situation and previous responses.

title: Embryo Transfer
description: The moment you've been working toward - placing your embryo(s) into your uterus. It's a quick, usually painless procedure similar to a pap smear, but emotionally significant as you begin the two-week wait.

options.label: Single embryo transfer
options.description: Transferring one embryo to minimize multiple pregnancy risks.

options.label: Double embryo transfer
options.description: Transferring two embryos, sometimes done with older women or lower grade embryos.

community_note: Try to stay relaxed during the procedure and remember that bed rest afterward isn't necessary - normal activity is fine.

title: Two Week Wait
description: The emotionally challenging period between transfer and pregnancy test. You'll likely analyze every symptom and struggle with the urge to test early. This is one of the hardest parts of the IVF process for most women.

options.label: Wait for official beta test
options.description: Avoiding home pregnancy tests and waiting for clinic's blood test.

options.label: Home testing during wait
options.description: Using home pregnancy tests before the official beta test.

community_note: There's no right way to handle the two week wait - do whatever helps you cope while staying as busy and distracted as possible.

title: Pregnancy Test Results
description: Getting your beta HCG results to determine if the transfer was successful. This can be the most emotionally intense moment of your entire IVF journey, whether the news is positive or negative.

options.label: Positive pregnancy test
options.description: Successful implantation confirmed by rising HCG levels.

options.label: Negative or failed implantation
options.description: Transfer unsuccessful, requiring decisions about next steps.

options.label: Chemical pregnancy
options.description: Brief positive that ends in early miscarriage.

community_note: Remember that IVF often takes multiple attempts - a negative result doesn't mean you won't ultimately succeed.
